title: The Three Holy Hierarchs, St. Peter of Sebaste, St. Macrina the Younger & St. Naucratius
narrative:
The Three Holy Hierarchs, St. Peter of Sebaste, St. Macrina the Younger, and St. Naucratius the Ascetic

On the twentieth day of Yekatit, the great feast of the Three Holy Hierarchs — Basil the Great, Gregory the Theologian, and John Chrysostom — is enriched by the presence of three figures from the same extraordinary Cappadocian family and its circle, whose inclusion transforms this commemoration from a celebration of famous doctors into a meditation on the whole community of formation and mutual sanctification that produced them. The Three Hierarchs are set before the faithful as the supreme doctors of the Greek-speaking Church: Basil the legislator of monasticism and the great bishop of Caesarea, Gregory the theologian whose orations on the Holy Trinity remain unsurpassed in the history of Christian doctrine, and John Chrysostom the preacher of Antioch and patriarch of Constantinople whose homilies have never been replaced in the treasury of scriptural exposition. Together they represent the integration of the active and contemplative lives at the highest level of human achievement.

Saint Macrina the Younger — elder sister of Basil and Gregory and the person the tradition credits most directly with forming their holiness — is the great hidden figure of this feast. It was Macrina who, after the death of their father, persuaded the family to transform their estate at Annisa in Pontus into a monastic community in which all social distinctions were dissolved, who guided the formation of the young Basil when he returned from Athens inflated with philosophical self-confidence, and who nursed Gregory through the grief of her and Basil's deaths in her final days. Gregory's account of her last hours — the Life of Macrina — is one of the most moving documents of patristic hagiography, and in it he calls her simply the Teacher, without qualification. The Ethiopian Synaxarium includes her in this assembly as a reminder that behind the visible achievements of the celebrated doctors stands the invisible labour of the woman who shaped them.

Saint Peter of Sebaste was the youngest of the ten children of the holy Basil the Elder and Macrina the Elder, the brother of Basil, Gregory, and Macrina the Younger, who received his formation entirely within the family's monastic household and eventually became Bishop of Sebaste — the same city that had witnessed the martyrdom of the Forty Soldiers. Peter is the least written-about of the siblings, but the tradition notes that his episcopal ministry was marked by an extraordinary gentleness and pastoral closeness to the poor, as though the great theological ambitions of his older brothers had been distilled in him into pure pastoral charity. He visited the sick personally, fasted with the hungry, and was known throughout his province as the bishop who never turned anyone away.

Saint Naucratius, the brother who is almost never mentioned, is the discovery of this day's assembly: the eldest son of the family who, before Basil and Gregory had begun their studies, had already renounced his considerable worldly prospects, retreated to the banks of the Iris River in Pontus with a single companion, and spent his years in prayer, manual labour, and the care of the elderly poor of the surrounding countryside. He died in a fishing accident before any of his siblings had become famous, and his death was the sorrow that accelerated Macrina's decision to transform the family estate into a monastery. Naucratius appears nowhere in the standard accounts of Cappadocian Christianity, yet it was his example — the eldest son who gave everything up first, before it was fashionable, before the family had a monastic programme — that made everything that followed possible. The Three Hierarchs receive their doctorates this day in the company of the sister who taught them and the brother no one remembers, and the feast is the richer for it. Amen.
